title.en: Cloud Operations Suite: Logging, Monitoring, Tracing

description.en: Instrument GCP workloads with Cloud Logging, Monitoring, and Trace; design SLOs with error budgets and burn-rate alerts that page on user pain, not noise.

story_variants.en:
## You Can't Fix What You Can't See

Everything this track built — Cloud Run services, Pub/Sub fanouts, BigQuery pipelines, Vertex endpoints — emits telemetry into the **Cloud Operations Suite** (the artist formerly known as Stackdriver). Observability is the connective tissue: the difference between 'users say it's slow' and 'p99 latency on revision api-00042 regressed at 14:03, correlated with the model rollout, here's the trace.'

The suite's three pillars map to the three questions of any incident:

- **Cloud Logging** — *what happened?* Structured event records.
- **Cloud Monitoring** — *how much / how often?* Time-series metrics, dashboards, alerting.
- **Cloud Trace** — *where did the time go?* Request paths across services.

## Logging: Structured or Sorry

GCP services log automatically (Cloud Run request logs, GKE container stdout, BigQuery job logs, and the all-important **Audit Logs** recording who did what to which resource). Your applications should log **structured JSON** — one line per event with `severity`, `message`, and your own fields — because every downstream capability keys on structure:

- The **Logs Explorer** queries fields, not regexes over prose.
- **Log-based metrics** turn matching entries into time series ('rate of checkout_failed by region').
- **Log sinks** route entries: to BigQuery for analysis (lesson 6), to GCS for retention (lesson 3), to Pub/Sub for automation (lesson 5). Sinks at folder or org level, configured by the landing zone (lesson 13), are how enterprises get tamper-evident central audit trails.

Severity levels are a contract, not decoration: DEBUG (development detail), INFO (business-as-usual events), WARNING (unexpected but handled), ERROR (a request failed), CRITICAL (the service is failing). Alerting logic — including the challenge you'll build — depends on teams honoring that contract.

> **Pitfall:** Logging unstructured prose ('Something went wrong in payment!!') costs the same to ingest as structured JSON but is worth a fraction as much. Ingestion is billed per volume too — DEBUG-at-production-volume is a FinOps line item wearing an observability costume.

## Monitoring: Metrics, Dashboards, and the Art of Not Paging

Cloud Monitoring collects platform metrics automatically (Cloud Run instance counts, Pub/Sub `oldest_unacked_message_age`, Vertex endpoint prediction latencies) and accepts custom metrics via OpenTelemetry. Dashboards are table stakes; **alerting policy design** is the discipline:

| Alert on | Example | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Symptoms (user pain) | Elevated error ratio, p99 latency | Pages a human only when users hurt |
| Causes (system state) | CPU 85%, backlog growing | Tickets/dashboards — investigation fodder, not pages |
| Absence | No heartbeat log in 10 min | Catches silent death, the scariest failure |

## SLOs and Error Budgets: Alerting Like an SRE

Google's SRE practice, productized in Cloud Monitoring's SLO features, reframes reliability: define an **SLI** (a measurable ratio, e.g. successful requests / total), target an **SLO** (say 99% over 30 days), and treat the remainder — 1% — as the **error budget** you may spend on releases, experiments, and bad luck.

The alerting magic is the **burn rate**: how fast you're consuming budget relative to plan. Burn rate 1.0 means exactly on-budget; 4.0 means the whole month's budget gone in a week. Production setups alert on burn rate at multiple windows — fast-burn (page: high rate over minutes) and slow-burn (ticket: moderate rate over hours) — which kills both classic failure modes: paging on one blip, and snoozing through a slow leak that torches the month.

> **Callout:** The error budget is also a management protocol: budget left means ship faster; budget exhausted means freeze features and fix reliability. It converts an eternal engineering argument into arithmetic.

## Trace: The Third Pillar

Distributed tracing propagates a context ID across service hops (Cloud Run -> Pub/Sub -> function -> Vertex endpoint), so the 900ms user request decomposes into spans: 40ms gateway, 120ms retrieval, 700ms model inference. Instrument once with **OpenTelemetry** — the vendor-neutral standard GCP now recommends — and export to Cloud Trace. For this track's LLM workloads, traces answer the question dashboards can't: *which stage* of the RAG pipeline (lesson 12) is eating the latency budget.

## Closing the Loop on the Whole Track

Notice the pattern of the enterprise_patterns module: landing zones make environments consistent, FinOps makes spend accountable, observability makes behavior legible — and all three are wired with the same primitives you learned early: IAM, Pub/Sub, BigQuery, GCS. Platform engineering is compositional. So is this final challenge.

## What You'll Build

An SLO burn-rate monitor: aggregate a log stream by severity, compute the error ratio (ERROR + CRITICAL over total), derive the burn rate against a 99% SLO's 1% error budget, and decide — PAGE or OK. It is the arithmetic inside every SRE pager, reduced to twenty deterministic lines. Ship it, and you've completed the track.

description.en: Logs become metrics, metrics become SLOs, SLOs become the only pages worth waking for.

animation_sequence.code:
import json, sys
log = {"severity": "ERROR",
       "message": "checkout failed",
       "order_id": "o-7741", "region": "eu"}
print(json.dumps(log), file=sys.stdout)

explanation.en: severity is the field the entire alerting chain keys on — honor the contract.
Your own fields become queryable dimensions in Logs Explorer and log-based metrics.
On Cloud Run/GKE, one JSON object per stdout line is automatically parsed into a structured entry.

left.label: Page the on-call when CPU hits 85%

right.label: Page on SLO burn rate; route CPU alerts to a dashboard/ticket

description.en: Symptoms page, causes inform. Guard the pager's signal-to-noise like an SLO of its own.
The burn-rate alert your challenge computes — budget spent 4x faster than sustainable.
From a JSON line to a resolved incident — the full observability loop.

instruction.en: Build an SLO burn-rate monitor. LOGS is a list of severity strings. Implement count_by_severity(logs) returning a dict of counts; error_ratio(counts) returning (ERROR + CRITICAL) / total; and burn_rate(ratio, error_budget) returning ratio / error_budget. The SLO is 99% (error budget 0.01). Print counts in the fixed order INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL as '<SEVERITY>: <count>', then 'error ratio: <pct>%' (2 decimals), then 'burn rate: <x>x -> PAGE' if the burn rate exceeds 1 else 'burn rate: <x>x -> OK' (1 decimal). Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
LOGS = ["INFO"] * 90 + ["WARNING"] * 6 + ["ERROR"] * 3 + ["CRITICAL"] * 1
SEVERITY_ORDER = ["INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR", "CRITICAL"]
ERROR_BUDGET = 0.01  # 99% SLO

def count_by_severity(logs):
    # TODO: return {severity: count} covering every severity in SEVERITY_ORDER (0 if absent)
    pass

def error_ratio(counts):
    # TODO: (ERROR + CRITICAL) / total entries
    pass

def burn_rate(ratio, error_budget):
    # TODO: ratio / error_budget
    pass

counts = count_by_severity(LOGS)
# TODO: print '<SEV>: <count>' for each severity in SEVERITY_ORDER
# TODO: ratio = error_ratio(counts); print 'error ratio: <ratio*100:.2f>%'
# TODO: br = burn_rate(ratio, ERROR_BUDGET)
#       print 'burn rate: <br:.1f>x -> PAGE' if br > 1 else 'burn rate: <br:.1f>x -> OK'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
INFO: 90
WARNING: 6
ERROR: 3
CRITICAL: 1
error ratio: 4.00%
burn rate: 4.0x -> PAGE

en: count_by_severity: start from {s: 0 for s in SEVERITY_ORDER} then increment per log entry.
burn rate = 0.04 / 0.01 = 4.0; since 4.0 > 1 the verdict is PAGE. Format with :.1f.

practice_challenges.test_code:
_c = count_by_severity(LOGS)
assert _c == {"INFO": 90, "WARNING": 6, "ERROR": 3, "CRITICAL": 1}
assert abs(error_ratio(_c) - 0.04) < 1e-9
assert abs(burn_rate(0.04, 0.01) - 4.0) < 1e-9
assert abs(burn_rate(0.005, 0.01) - 0.5) < 1e-9
_c2 = count_by_severity(["INFO", "INFO"])
assert _c2["ERROR"] == 0 and _c2["CRITICAL"] == 0
assert abs(error_ratio(_c2) - 0.0) < 1e-9

q.en: Which pillar answers 'where did the 900ms go across my Cloud Run -> Vertex endpoint request path?'
Why log structured JSON instead of prose?

en: Fields become queryable in Logs Explorer and drive log-based metrics, sinks, and alerts

q.en: A service has a 99.9% SLO. What is its error budget?

en: 0.1% of requests over the SLO window

q.en: A burn rate of 4.0 sustained means:

en: The service is four times faster than needed
The month's error budget will be exhausted in about a quarter of the window — fast-burn territory, page now
Nothing; burn rates over 1 are normal forever
The SLO target should be lowered immediately
